title: Intel 8086 Microprocessor Architecture
examTip: BIU handles bus operations (fetch + address generation). EU handles execution. They work in parallel — BIU pre-fetches next instruction while EU executes current one. 20-bit address = Segment × 16 + Offset → 1MB memory space.

children.label: Arithmetic & Logic Unit (ALU)
sublabels: 16-bit operations: ADD SUB MUL DIV AND OR XOR

children.label: Flag Register (16-bit)
sublabels: CF  PF  AF  ZF  SF  TF  IF  DF  OF

buses.label: 20-bit Address Bus  (A0 – A19)  →  1 MB Memory Space
Control Bus  (RD, WR, IO/M, ALE, READY, RESET, INTR, INTA, CLK)

theory:
The Intel 8086 is a 16-bit microprocessor introduced in 1978, the foundation of the modern x86 architecture. It operates at 5–10 MHz and features a 20-bit address bus providing 1 MB of addressable memory (2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes). Internally, the 8086 architecture is divided into two independent, pipelined functional units: the Bus Interface Unit (BIU) and the Execution Unit (EU).

Bus Interface Unit (BIU): The BIU manages all communication between the processor and external memory/I/O. It contains four 16-bit segment registers — Code Segment (CS), Data Segment (DS), Stack Segment (SS), and Extra Segment (ES) — which divide the 1 MB address space into 64 KB segments. Physical addresses are computed by the 16-bit Adder using the formula: Physical Address = (Segment Register × 16) + Offset. The Instruction Pointer (IP) holds the 16-bit offset of the next instruction within the code segment. The 6-byte Instruction Queue (FIFO) pre-fetches up to 6 bytes of instruction code from memory whenever the external bus is idle, enabling instruction pipelining.

Execution Unit (EU): The EU decodes and executes instructions fed by the BIU queue without accessing the bus directly. It contains eight 16-bit General Purpose Registers: AX (Accumulator — used in I/O, multiply, divide), BX (Base — used in memory addressing), CX (Counter — used in loops and shifts), DX (Data — used in I/O and 32-bit operations), and four pointer/index registers: SP (Stack Pointer), BP (Base Pointer), SI (Source Index), DI (Destination Index). The AX, BX, CX, DX registers can each be accessed as two independent 8-bit halves (AH/AL, BH/BL, CH/CL, DH/DL). The 16-bit ALU performs all arithmetic and logic operations. The Flag Register contains 9 active flags: CF (Carry), PF (Parity), AF (Auxiliary Carry), ZF (Zero), SF (Sign), TF (Trap), IF (Interrupt Enable), DF (Direction), OF (Overflow).

Pipelining: While the EU executes the current instruction, the BIU simultaneously pre-fetches the next instruction bytes into the queue, achieving instruction-level parallelism and improving throughput significantly.
keyPoints: 8086 is a 16-bit processor with 20-bit address bus → addresses 1 MB (2^20) of memory
Physical Address = Segment Register × 16 + Offset (the 16-bit Adder in BIU computes this)
6-byte instruction queue in BIU enables pipelining — BIU fetches while EU executes
General Purpose Registers: AX, BX, CX, DX (each split into 8-bit H and L halves), SP, BP, SI, DI
9 active flags in flag register: CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, TF, IF, DF, OF
useCases: Foundation of x86 PC architecture — all modern Intel/AMD processors are backward compatible
Embedded systems and industrial controllers using legacy x86 code
Educational reference for understanding segmented memory, pipelining, and register-based architecture
